draft.introduction: The vine and branches discourse of John 15:1–8 is the most sustained organic metaphor for divine-human union in the New Testament. Jesus does not say the disciples are like branches; he says they are branches, and he is the vine. The relationship is not analogy but identity—the same life flows through vine and branch. The Greek verb meno (μένω), “to remain” or “abide,” appears eleven times in these eight verses, hammering home the necessity of continuous indwelling. The passage is both promise and warning. Those who abide in the vine bear fruit; those who do not are cut off and burned. The Fathers read this as a description of the mechanics of theosis: participation in the divine life is not a static achievement but an ongoing relationship that requires active cooperation. Separation from the vine is not merely moral failure but ontological catastrophe—a severing from the source of life itself.

translation.text: I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser. Every branch in me that does not bear fruit he takes away, and every branch that bears fruit he prunes, so that it may bear more fruit.
translation.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος ἡ ἀληθινή · καὶ ὁ πατήρ μου ὁ γεωργός ἐστιν · πᾶν κλῆμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μὴ φέρον καρπόν · αἴρει αὐτό · καὶ πᾶν τὸ καρπὸν φέρον · καθαίρει αὐτό · ἵνα καρπὸν πλείονα φέρῃ ·

translation.text: Already you are clean because of the word that I have spoken to you. Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit by itself unless it abides in the vine, neither can you unless you abide in me.
translation.original_text: ἤδη ὑμεῖς καθαροί ἐστε διὰ τὸν λόγον ὃν λελάληκα ὑμῖν · μείνατε ἐν ἐμοί · κἀγὼ ἐν ὑμῖν · καθὼς τὸ κλῆμα οὐ δύναται καρπὸν φέρειν ἀφʼ ἑαυτοῦ ἐὰν μὴ μένῃ ἐν τῇ ἀμπέλῳ · οὕτως οὐδὲ ὑμεῖς ἐὰν μὴ ἐν ἐμοὶ μένητε ·

translation.text: I am the vine, you are the branches. The one who abides in me and I in him, this one bears much fruit, because apart from me you can do nothing. If anyone does not abide in me, he is thrown out like the branch and withers; and they gather them and throw them into the fire, and they are burned.
translation.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος · ὑμεῖς τὰ κλήματα · ὁ μένων ἐν ἐμοὶ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ · οὗτος φέρει καρπὸν πολύν · ὅτι χωρὶς ἐμοῦ οὐ δύνασθε ποιεῖν οὐδέν · ἐὰν μή τις μένῃ ἐν ἐμοί · ἐβλήθη ἔξω ὡς τὸ κλῆμα καὶ ἐξηράνθη · καὶ συνάγουσιν αὐτὰ καὶ εἰς τὸ πῦρ βάλλουσιν · καὶ καίεται ·

translation.text: If you abide in me, and my words abide in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you. By this my Father is glorified, that you bear much fruit and become my disciples.
translation.original_text: ἐὰν μείνητε ἐν ἐμοὶ καὶ τὰ ῥήματά μου ἐν ὑμῖν μείνῃ · ὃ ἐὰν θέλητε αἰτήσασθε · καὶ γενήσεται ὑμῖν · ἐν τούτῳ ἐδοξάσθη ὁ πατήρ μου · ἵνα καρπὸν πολὺν φέρητε καὶ γένησθε ἐμοὶ μαθηταί ·

translator_notes.note: The “I am” (ego eimi, ἐγώ εἰμι) formula echoes the divine name revealed at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14, LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν). This is the seventh and final “I am” saying in John’s Gospel. The vine metaphor draws on a rich Old Testament tradition in which Israel is God’s vineyard (Isaiah 5:1–7, Psalm 80:8–16). Jesus claims not merely to tend the vine but to be it.

The verb meno (μένω) is the key theological term in this passage. It means to remain, abide, or dwell. In Johannine theology, it describes the permanent mutual indwelling of Father and Son (14:10), of Christ and the believer (here), and of the Spirit in the community (14:17). The concept is not static but dynamic—it implies continuous, active participation in the life of the vine.
